title: Analyzing the eLife Fitness Dataset with MPCurver
text: vignette shows use public fit_mpcurve() interface eLife fitness dataset. goal just produce fit, explain modeling logic step step. use MPCurver answer three practical questions: Can single latent ordering explain mutant fitness across environments? encourage smoother trajectories, keep ordering improving interpretability? data support multiple ordering systems, single ordering remain sufficient account measurement error? recommended workflow throughout default cavi backend exposed fit_mpcurve().

title: Analyzing the eLife Fitness Dataset with MPCurver
text: Just using error columns true SEs might risky, adjust SEs using t-statistics-adjustment: dataset: row mutant; column environment; matrix entry fitness score; paired *_error columns provide observation-level measurement standard deviations can optionally pass fit_mpcurve() via S.
code: if (requireNamespace("MPCurver", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(MPCurver) } else if (requireNamespace("devtools", quietly = TRUE)) {   devtools::load_all(".", quiet = TRUE) } else {   stop("Need either an installed MPCurver package or devtools::load_all().") }  set.seed(42)  locate_elife_csv <- function() {   pkg_path <- system.file("extdata", "elife-61271-fig2-data1-v2.csv", package = "MPCurver")   if (nzchar(pkg_path) && file.exists(pkg_path)) {     return(pkg_path)   }    src_path <- file.path("inst", "extdata", "elife-61271-fig2-data1-v2.csv")   if (file.exists(src_path)) {     return(src_path)   }    stop("Could not locate elife-61271-fig2-data1-v2.csv.") }  csv_path <- locate_elife_csv() dat <- read.csv(csv_path, check.names = FALSE)  fitness_cols <- grep("_fitness$", names(dat), value = TRUE) error_cols <- grep("_error$", names(dat), value = TRUE)  X <- as.matrix(dat[, fitness_cols, drop = FALSE]) mode(X) <- "numeric" colnames(X) <- sub("_fitness$", "", fitness_cols)  S <- as.matrix(dat[, error_cols, drop = FALSE]) mode(S) <- "numeric" colnames(S) <- sub("_error$", "", error_cols)  meta <- dat[, c("barcode", "gene", "type", "ploidy", "class", "mutation_type"), drop = FALSE] t_adjust_se <- function(Y, S, nu, tol = 1e-12) {   if (!is.matrix(Y) || !is.numeric(Y)) {     stop("`Y` must be a numeric matrix.")   }   if (!is.matrix(S) || !is.numeric(S)) {     stop("`S` must be a numeric matrix.")   }   if (!all(dim(Y) == dim(S))) {     stop("`Y` and `S` must have the same dimensions.")   }   if (any(S < 0, na.rm = TRUE)) {     stop("`S` must be nonnegative.")   }      if (length(nu) == 1L) {     if (!is.numeric(nu) || is.na(nu) || nu <= 0) {       stop("`nu` must be a positive number.")     }     nu_mat <- matrix(nu, nrow = nrow(Y), ncol = ncol(Y))   } else {     if (!is.matrix(nu) || !is.numeric(nu) || !all(dim(nu) == dim(Y))) {       stop("`nu` must be either a positive scalar or a numeric matrix with the same dimensions as `Y`.")     }     if (any(nu <= 0, na.rm = TRUE)) {       stop("All entries of `nu` must be positive.")     }     nu_mat <- nu   }      z_t <- Y / S      # Main calculation:   # S_tilde = S * z_t / qnorm(pt(z_t, df = nu))   p_t <- pt(z_t, df = nu_mat)   z_n <- qnorm(p_t)      S_tilde <- S * z_t / z_n      # Numerical fix for z_t close to 0:   # limit of z / qnorm(pt(z, df=nu)) as z -> 0 is dnorm(0) / dt(0, df=nu)   near_zero <- is.finite(z_t) & abs(z_t) < tol   if (any(near_zero, na.rm = TRUE)) {     limit_factor <- dnorm(0) / dt(0, df = nu_mat[near_zero])     S_tilde[near_zero] <- S[near_zero] * limit_factor   }      # Preserve zeros   zero_se <- (S == 0) & !is.na(S)   if (any(zero_se)) {     S_tilde[zero_se] <- 0   }      dimnames(S_tilde) <- dimnames(S)   S_tilde } S <- t_adjust_se(X, S, nu = 4)

title: Analyzing the eLife Fitness Dataset with MPCurver
text: start simplest question: can one latent ordering explain main structure dataset? call uses main user-facing API: method = "isomap" supplies initialization; K = 50 uses 50-bin latent grid; rw_q = 2 uses second-order random-walk prior smooth trajectories.
code: fit_unidir <- fit_mpcurve(   X,   method = "isomap",   K = 50,   rw_q = 2,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Analyzing the eLife Fitness Dataset with MPCurver
text: now refit single-ordering model, add stronger prior lambda_j lambda_sd_prior_rate. Larger values encourage smoother trajectories.   curves easier read: global ordering remains, fitted environment trajectories less wiggly. useful sanity check whether smoothing changes inferred mutant ordering , mainly changes trajectory estimates.  dataset two fits stay close, reassuring: stronger regularization mostly improves interpretability rather changing basic ordering story.
code: fit_unidir_smooth <- fit_mpcurve(   X,   method = "isomap",   K = 50,   iter = 500,   rw_q = 2,   lambda_sd_prior_rate = 1e4,   verbose = FALSE ) plot(fit_unidir_smooth, plot_type = "elbo") fit_unidir_smooth #> MPCurve fit #>   Backend        : cavi #>   Model          : homoskedastic #>   n / d / K : 421 / 45 / 50 #>   Iterations     : 349 #>   Position prior : adaptive #>   Converged      : yes #>   ELBO (last)   : 11842.508096 #>   logLik (last): 14069.441003 par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1)) plot(fit_unidir_smooth, dims = 1) plot(fit_unidir_smooth, dims = 3) plot(fit_unidir_smooth, dims = 7) plot(fit_unidir_smooth, dims = 9) par(mfrow = c(1, 1)) cor(   fit_unidir_smooth$locations$mean$pseudotime,   fit_unidir$locations$mean$pseudotime ) #> [1] 0.952165  plot(   fit_unidir_smooth$locations$mean$pseudotime,   fit_unidir$locations$mean$pseudotime,   xlab = "Latent position (smoother fit)",   ylab = "Latent position (less regularized fit)" ) abline(0, 1, lty = 2)

what: Step 3: Ask whether one ordering is enough
title: Analyzing the eLife Fitness Dataset with MPCurver
text: single-ordering fit assumes every environment varies smoothly along latent mutant axis. may restrictive. environments may reflect different adaptation program therefore prefer different latent ordering. test idea, let fit_mpcurve() search four latent ordering systems using greedy backward selection. model starts four orderings prunes back optimal number based ELBO.
code: fit_partition <- fit_mpcurve(   X,   method = "isomap",   intrinsic_dim = 4,   greedy = "backward",   ridge = 1e-6,   K = 50,   iter = 500,   rw_q = 2,   lambda_sd_prior_rate = 1e4,   verbose = FALSE ) fit_partition #> MPCurve partition fit #>   Backend        : cavi #>   Greedy search  : backward (upper bound 4 -> selected 3) #>   Dim (req/act/view): 3 / 3 / 3 #>   n / d / K      : 421 / 45 / 50 #>   Partition      : A=35, B=1, C=9 #>   Active         : A, B, C #>   Position prior : adaptive #>   Partition prior: adaptive #>   Objective (last): 12526.762490 #>   Objective type : fixed-requested-M comparison #>   Converged      : TRUE #>   Phase-2 rel_delta   : 9.154e-06 #>   Small-step streak: 3 #>   Reason         : Phase-2 rel_delta stayed below 1.0e-05 for 3 consecutive nonnegative steps.

title: Feature Partitioning with MPCurver

title: Feature Partitioning with MPCurver
text: Dimension selection now unified partition-CAVI workflow fit_mpcurve(greedy = ...). greedy = "forward" treats intrinsic_dim upper bound compares M versus M + 1 sequentially starting M = 1. greedy = "backward" first fits upper bound, removes one active ordering time compares M versus M - 1. greedy modes always compare models using fixed-requested-M comparison objective, internally run drop_unused_ordering = FALSE even ask compact final returned view. True vs inferred feature partitions.
code: fit_forward <- fit_mpcurve(   X,   intrinsic_dim = 4,   greedy = "forward",   K = 16,   drop_unused_ordering = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )  fit_backward <- fit_mpcurve(   X,   intrinsic_dim = 4,   greedy = "backward",   K = 16,   drop_unused_ordering = TRUE,   verbose = FALSE ) summary_tbl <- data.frame(   fit = c("soft (fixed M=2)", "greedy forward", "greedy backward"),   displayed_dim = c(     fit_partition$intrinsic_dim,     fit_forward$intrinsic_dim,     fit_backward$intrinsic_dim   ),   selected_dim = c(     fit_partition$requested_intrinsic_dim,     fit_forward$greedy_selection$selected_intrinsic_dim,     fit_backward$greedy_selection$selected_intrinsic_dim   ) ) print(summary_tbl, row.names = FALSE) #>               fit displayed_dim selected_dim #>  soft (fixed M=2)             2            2 #>    greedy forward             2            2 #>   greedy backward             2            2 assignment_heatmap(   truth    = truth,   soft     = fit_partition$partition$assign,   forward  = fit_forward$partition$assign,   backward = fit_backward$partition$assign,   main = "Feature partition comparison" )

Greedy search always compares models fixed-M objective. Start intrinsic_dim = 2. partition unclear, consider M=3M = 3 allow third ordering capture residual structure. Specify independent init methods. method = c("fiedler", "PCA") uses spectral ordering first axis PC1 second explicit PCA-based ordering. want later PCA axes explicit starts, pass pca_components directly. Even inferred orderings imperfect, feature partition can still accurate. Check objective trace. Within one fixed intrinsic_dim, sustained decreases red flag; one-step drop freeze-affected iteration possible excluded convergence accounting. Compare across intrinsic_dim drop_unused_ordering = FALSE. mode stored objective keeps fixed requested-M comparison semantics. drop_unused_ordering = TRUE, becomes post-drop fit objective compared across requested dimensions.

Used partition_prior = "fixed". omitted, fixed prior defaults uniform active orderings. assignment_prior Deprecated compatibility argument old partition-prior API. "uniform" maps partition_prior = "fixed" uniform prior. T_start, T_end Annealing temperatures partition CAVI. n_outer Number annealing steps partition CAVI. inner_iter Number weighted CAVI sweeps per annealing step. max_converge_iter Maximum number exact T = 1 partition iterations annealing. Defaults iter NULL. tol_outer Relative objective tolerance partition phase-2 convergence. freeze_unused_ordering partition fits : TRUE, inactive orderings frozen skipped subsequent weighted updates. freeze_unused_ordering_threshold partition fits : non-negative feature-mass threshold used decide ordering effectively unused. freeze_feature partition fits : TRUE, feature-ordering pairs sufficiently small posterior weight frozen trajectory contribution neutralized. freeze_feature_weight_threshold partition fits : non-negative threshold w_{jm} used freeze_feature = TRUE. drop_unused_ordering partition fits : TRUE, returned mpcurve view may compact away frozen orderings. what: Simulate a two-ordering GP dataset (Matern) for feature partitioning ‚Äî simulate_two_order_gp_dataset
title: Simulate a two-ordering GP dataset (Matern) for feature partitioning ‚Äî simulate_two_order_gp_dataset
text: Simulates \(N \times D\) dataset two latent sample orderings \(t_1\) \(t_2\). first \(D/2\) features generated Gaussian process \(t_1\), remaining \(D/2\) features generated independent Gaussian process \(t_2\). feature GP draw evaluated \(N\) sample locations. Optionally: permute feature columns (permute_cols), permute sample order within block 2 (permute_rows_block2), shift data positive (shift_positive), add ..d. Gaussian noise (noise_sd). Column names assigned ** permutation permuted consistently columns, names remain aligned returned true_group.

Range sampling t1 t2. range, smoothness, variance Matern GP hyperparameters. noise_sd Nonnegative numeric. Standard deviation ..d. Gaussian noise added X. shift_positive Logical; TRUE, shift GP block minimum 1. permute_cols Logical; TRUE, permute feature columns. permute_rows_block2 Logical; TRUE, permute rows second GP block combining. seed Optional integer seed.
